title: 1.5 Stacks & Queues
summary: You already know the concept; the gap is Python. New tools: authoring your own class (__init__, self, methods), list as a stack (append / pop() / [-1]), and collections.deque as a queue (append / popleft() / [0]). Worked solutions for the Stack and Queue classes are in collapsed sections — study, close, reproduce. valid_parentheses is a real algorithm rep.
sessionNotes: Reformatted to syntax-first per user feedback: concept ≈ zero (collapsed), body is the Python toolkit aimed at the exercises, each with how + why. Full worked solutions live in collapsed sections for the study→close→reproduce method. E2 left as genuine problem-solving (no solution given). Scoped to exclude already-documented syntax. RESULT (Session 10, 2026-06-03, re-done after the originals were lost): all 3 pass — E1 Stack & E3 Queue reproduced cleanly from the worked examples; E2 valid_parentheses (the prior genuine failure) solved independently and correctly, both edge guards handled, using match/case. The study→close→reproduce format worked.
intro: You know stacks & queues already — the only real gap is Python: writing your OWN class, and knowing which list/deque method does what. This lesson is that toolkit. Concept refresher is collapsed below; open it only if you want it.

body.heading: Concept refresher — LIFO / FIFO (you've got this; open if you want)

blocks.content: STACK = LIFO: add and remove at ONE end (the top). QUEUE = FIFO: add at the back, remove from the front. Use a plain list for a stack. Use collections.deque for a queue, because a list's pop(0) shifts every element left → O(n). Trigger: nesting / undo → stack; arrival-order / BFS → queue.

body.heading: What's actually new here: writing your own class
body.content:
You've USED a class (ListNode) but not authored one with methods. Three things to know:

• __init__(self) is the setup method — it runs once when you call Stack(). Put the object's starting state here.
• self means "this particular object." Every method takes it as the first parameter, and you never pass it yourself: s.push(3) automatically becomes push(s, 3).
• self._items stores state ON the object. The leading underscore is just a convention meaning "internal — don't poke at it from outside."

body.heading: Class skeleton (the shape every method follows)
Tools for E1 — a list IS a stack (all O(1))
body.content:
push     ->  self._items.append(val)
  pop      ->  return self._items.pop()    # no arg = remove+return LAST
  peek     ->  return self._items[-1]      # last item, no removal
  is_empty ->  return not self._items      # an empty list is falsy

  Why the end is cheap: the last slot of an array needs no shifting -> O(1).

body.heading: E1 worked example — full Stack (study it, then CLOSE this and write it yourself)

blocks.content: Read each line until it makes sense, then collapse this section and reproduce it from memory in Exercise 1 below. Peeking-while-typing doesn't stick — the reconstruction is where it sticks.

body.heading: Tools for E3 — a deque IS a queue (all O(1))
body.content:
enqueue  ->  self._items.append(val)
  dequeue  ->  return self._items.popleft()   # remove+return the FRONT
  peek     ->  return self._items[0]          # front item, no removal
  is_empty ->  return not self._items

  Why deque, not a list: list.pop(0) shifts every element left -> O(n) each,
  so draining n items is O(n^2). deque is O(1) at BOTH ends. (Set it up with
  self._items = deque() in __init__.)

body.heading: E3 worked example — full Queue (study, close, reproduce)

blocks.content: Same drill: understand it, collapse it, then write Exercise 3 from memory.

body.heading: E2 is a real rep — no worked solution given
body.content:
valid_parentheses is genuine problem-solving, so there's no answer to study — that's the point. Syntax you might reach for: a dict pairs = {')':'(', ']':'[', '}':'{'}, a membership test like ch in '([{', and a list as a stack. The three decisions inside the loop:

  1) ch is an opener  -> push it.
  2) ch is a closer   -> invalid if the stack is empty OR the popped top doesn't match:
                         if not stack or stack.pop() != pairs[ch]: return False
  3) after the loop   -> valid only if nothing is left open:  return not stack

The empty-stack guard in (2) is the piece people miss — e.g. ']' arriving with an empty stack.

exercises.title: E1 — Stack class (reproduce from the worked example)
exercises.prompt: Implement Stack with push(val), pop() (remove+return top), peek() (top without removing), is_empty() → bool, every op O(1). Study the worked example above, collapse it, then write this from memory. Run tests when done.
exercises.critique: Correct — all 3 tests pass, and you reproduced it cleanly from memory (exactly the point of the worked-example → close → rebuild method). Only difference from the model: is_empty uses `len(self._items) == 0`, which is 100% correct. The more Pythonic form is `return not self._items` (an empty list is falsy) — both read the same; worth keeping in your back pocket. Solid.
tests:
s = Stack()
assert s.is_empty() is True

s = Stack()
s.push(1)
s.push(2)
s.push(3)
assert s.is_empty() is False
assert s.peek() == 3

s = Stack()
s.push(1)
s.push(2)
s.push(3)
assert s.pop() == 3
assert s.pop() == 2
assert s.peek() == 1
assert s.pop() == 1
assert s.is_empty() is True

exercises.title: E2 — valid_parentheses (real algorithm rep)
exercises.prompt: Return True if every bracket in s (only ()[]{}) is closed by the matching type in the right order. '()[]{}'→True, '([)]'→False, ''→True. Use the three decisions from the lesson; no worked solution provided on purpose.
exercises.critique: This was the one that mattered — last time it was a genuine blank, and this time you nailed it independently. All 8 tests pass, including BOTH edge guards: empty stack on a closer (']' with nothing pushed → False) and leftover openers at the end ('(' → False). The logic is correct and very readable. Two notes: (1) match/case works fine here (Python 3.10+); for matching a single value it's effectively an if/elif, so the more common idiom is `if ch in '([{': ... elif ch in ')]}': ...` and it doesn't depend on 3.10. (2) Optional compaction: the whole closer branch collapses to `if not stack or stack.pop() != pairs[ch]: return False`. Your explicit version is arguably clearer for an interview — keep it if you prefer it. Excellent problem-solving rep.

exercises.title: E3 — Queue class (reproduce from the worked example)
exercises.prompt: Implement Queue with enqueue(val) (back), dequeue() (remove+return FRONT), peek() (front), is_empty(), every op O(1). Study the worked example, collapse it, then write from memory. deque is imported for you.
exercises.critique: Correct — all 3 tests pass, reproduced cleanly. You clearly internalized the list-vs-deque split: append to enqueue, popleft to dequeue, O(1) at both ends (a plain list would be O(n) per dequeue because pop(0) shifts everything). Same minor Pythonic note as E1: `return not self._items` reads a touch cleaner than `len(...) == 0`. Otherwise textbook.
tests:
q = Queue()
assert q.is_empty() is True

q = Queue()
q.enqueue('A')
q.enqueue('B')
q.enqueue('C')
assert q.is_empty() is False
assert q.peek() == 'A'

q = Queue()
q.enqueue('A')
q.enqueue('B')
q.enqueue('C')
assert q.dequeue() == 'A'
assert q.dequeue() == 'B'
assert q.peek() == 'C'
assert q.dequeue() == 'C'
assert q.is_empty() is True

reviewItemsAdded: Authoring a class: __init__(self) sets up state; every method takes self first; s.push(3) → push(s, 3)
list as a stack: append (push) / pop() (pop) / [-1] (peek) / not lst (is_empty)
deque as a queue: append (enqueue) / popleft() (dequeue) / [0] (peek); list pop(0) is O(n)
valid_parentheses: empty-stack guard on a closer (if not stack or stack.pop() != pairs[ch])

qa.q: What does `return not self._items` mean, and what is 'falsy'?
qa.a: Falsy = values Python treats as False in a boolean context (if / while / not / and / or). The falsy set: False, None, 0, 0.0, '' (empty string), and empty containers [], {}, set(), (). Everything else is truthy; for containers the rule of thumb is empty = falsy, non-empty = truthy. `not` flips truthiness into a real bool: not(falsy) = True, not(truthy) = False. So `return not self._items` asks 'is this list empty?' — empty list → falsy → not → True; list with items → truthy → not → False. Plain pseudocode: return True if the list has no items, else False. Identical result to `len(self._items) == 0`, just leaning on the container's own truthiness instead of counting. GOTCHA: truthiness lumps empty, 0, and None together, so only use `not x` when empty/zero/missing should ALL count as false. For a counter where 0 is a valid value, `if not count:` would wrongly fire on zero — use `== 0` or `is None` there (same family as the `is None` review item).

qa.q: What's Python's equivalent of a switch statement? (came up from the E2 solution)
qa.a: match / case (Python 3.10+). Multiple values share one branch via the or-pattern: `case '(' | '[' | '{':`. For matching a single simple value it's effectively an if/elif chain, so `if ch in '([{': ... elif ch in ')]}': ...` is the more common idiom and avoids the 3.10 dependency. Candidate for a flashcard under a new Python-syntax category (concept: python-match-case).
